coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) offer significant promise across industries due to their distinctive optical properties and strong biocompatibility. However, conventional synthesis methods frequently need more precision in controlling nanoparticle size, shape, and uniformity, limiting their versatility in diverse applications. Therefore, this study introduced an innovative approach to synthesising CQDs by combining electrochemical techniques with carbon dioxide (CO2) nanobubbles, circumventing the need for conventional surfactant-based exfoliation. According to the findings, the synthesised CQDs exhibited remarkable properties, including a high quantum yield of 26.17% and an average particle size of 2.24 ± 1.07 nm. Such characteristics render them exceptionally suitable for portable sensors for heavy metal detection in wastewater. Moreover, the investigation revealed the outstanding selectivity of the synthesised CQDs, particularly towards iron (Fe3+) ions, but demonstrated limited reactivity towards lead (Pb2+) and copper (Cu2+) ions. The novel approach also generated non-toxic CQD under the supply of CO2 nanobubbles, which offers a scalable avenue for developing advanced materials for various applications, including sensors for heavy metal ion detection in wastewater. The conventional synthesis method that relied on sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS) surfactants without CO2 nanobubbles yielded CQDs with a significantly lower quantum yield of 11.2%. In short, incorporating CO2 nanobubbles with electrochemical techniques enhanced quantum yield and superior control over the size and shape of the synthesized CQDs. This novel method successfully produced CQDs with scalable distinct morphologies and provided a new understanding of the CQD synthesis mechanism, with potential applications in portable sensing for environmental monitoring and water quality management.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological characterisations of the prepared CQDs. (A) HRTEM image of the CQDs. (B) High-resolution HRTEM image of a single CQD. (C) Particle size distribution of the CQDs analysed from HRTEM. (2-column fitting image).
Optical properties of the prepared CQDs. (A) UV-Vis spectra of the CQDs produced at different currents. (B) Photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission spectra of CQDs. (2-column fitting image).

Heavy metal sensing performance of the prepared CQDs. (A) Selectivity of the doped CQDs on different metal ions. (B) UV-Vis spectra of different doped CQDs/metal ion complexes. (C) Quenching efficiency of the doped CQDs against various Fe3+ concentrations. (D) Quenching efficiency of the doped CQDs against multiple pH levels. (E) Quenching efficiency of the doped CQDs under various temperatures. Note that the F and F
o represent the fluorescence emission of the doped CQDs before and after mixing with the metal ions. (2-column fitting image).

Performance of the 3D-printed portable doped CQDs-based sensor. (A) Graphical illustration of the 3D-printed portable sensor. (B) Normalised calibration plot between the fluorescence quenching and the Fe3+ concentration. (C) Stern-Volmer plot between the fluorescence quenching and Fe3+ concentration. (2-column fitting image).
Morphological characterisation of the electrolysed graphite surface via SEM imaging. (A) Before electrolysis and (B) After electrolysis. (2-column fitting image).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) offer significant promise across industries due to their distinctive optical properties and strong biocompatibility. However, conventional synthesis methods frequently need more precision in controlling nanoparticle size, shape, and uniformity, limiting their versatility in diverse applications. Therefore, this study introduced an innovative approach to synthesising CQDs by combining electrochemical techniques with carbon dioxide (CO2) nanobubbles, circumventing the need for conventional surfactant-based exfoliation. According to the findings, the synthesised CQDs exhibited remarkable properties, including a high quantum yield of 26.17% and an average particle size of 2.24 ± 1.07 nm. Such characteristics render them exceptionally suitable for portable sensors for heavy metal detection in wastewater. Moreover, the investigation revealed the outstanding selectivity of the synthesised CQDs, particularly towards iron (Fe3+) ions, but demonstrated limited reactivity towards lead (Pb2+) and copper (Cu2+) ions. The novel approach also generated non-toxic CQD under the supply of CO2 nanobubbles, which offers a scalable avenue for developing advanced materials for various applications, including sensors for heavy metal ion detection in wastewater. The conventional synthesis method that relied on sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS) surfactants without CO2 nanobubbles yielded CQDs with a significantly lower quantum yield of 11.2%. In short, incorporating CO2 nanobubbles with electrochemical techniques enhanced quantum yield and superior control over the size and shape of the synthesized CQDs. This novel method successfully produced CQDs with scalable distinct morphologies and provided a new understanding of the CQD synthesis mechanism, with potential applications in portable sensing for environmental monitoring and water quality management.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As human lifestyle improves, urbanisation and the rapid development of human civilisation have incurred the emergence of various manufacturing industries. In response, relevant manufacturing authorities are responsible for regulating the discharge of fatal and poisonous heavy metals into water sources, such as rivers and seas, which could result in detrimental health effects [1]. Notably, heavy metals are harmful elements with a density greater than 5 g/cm3 [2]. The most adverse impact of heavy metals is the biochemical disruption of the organic molecules via chelation in living organisms through cleavage formation using radicals, which ultimately degrade the biomolecules [3]. Over the past few decades, iron (Fe3+) ions have accumulated at full tilt compared to the other heavy metals globally [4]. As such, excessive intake of Fe3+ in humans would perniciously cause the malfunction of the renal organs, such as the liver, heart, and pancreas [2].

Recent interest has been focused on carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are novel carbonaceous nanoparticles that appear as a quasi-spherical shape with a size of less than 10 nm [5]. They have diverse structural morphologies, ranging from amorphous to crystalline structure, which intrinsically encapsulates a carbon core in the sp2 or sp3 hybridised state [6]. Based on past literature findings, CQDs could be conveniently synthesised via two general routes, namely, top-down and bottom-up approaches [7,8]. Nevertheless, these strategies typically consume a massive amount of energy and use strong chemicals with intricate synthesis conditions [9–11]. While numerous studies have been conducted to scrutinise the beneficial use of organic biomass as a novel raw material for energy-sustainable synthesis [12–14], their complex primitive carbon core structure might complicate the investigation of fluorescence origin of CQDs.

Eventually, research trends in CQDs have shifted towards the development of portable detection systems that could effectively quantify the targeted ions or molecules in a sample with real-time and on-site features [15]. Many studies have reported a common obstacle when quantifying the analytes using fluorescent paper sensors, whereby the accuracy and sensitivity features could not be determined through naked-eye speculation [16–19]. Interestingly, several studies that investigated the effect of carbon dioxide (CO2) nanobubbles in liquid-phase exfoliation of graphite into graphene layers recorded enhanced outcomes with the ionic interaction induced by the applied solvent [20–22]. Using molecular dynamic simulation, it was discovered that the origin of creating a sufficient interfacial force between the graphene layers via ionic solvents would be able to overcome the Van der Waals attraction that holds the graphene layers in structural fidelity, thus greatly improving the efficiency of the nanobubbles to intercalate them [23].

Meanwhile, numerous research efforts have been given to monitoring Fe3+ ions using simpler and more cost-effective ratiometric detection techniques as alternatives to the more complicated and time-consuming heavy metal ion detection techniques, such as Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS) [24–26]. Although the feasibility of complying with the quenching mechanisms in the fluorescence detection of heavy metal ions by non-toxic CQDs has been assessed incessantly with the strenuous considerations towards the optimisation of sensor selectivity and sensitivity [27–30], the stability and selectivity when integrating the CQDs into portable detection systems remain uncertain. Therefore, it is crucial to fabricate portable detection systems with a superior detection limit and stable performance for rapid Fe3+ ion monitoring in a complex detection environment with fluctuating temperature and pH levels.

Electrolysis is a safe, simple, and environmentally friendly chemical reaction under mild conditions [27,31,32]. Nevertheless, there are very few reports on the use of electrolysis to synthesise CQDs compared to other strategies, such as microwave-assisted and hydrothermal [33,34]. This situation prompts the idea to develop an innovative and effective scalable method for massive synthesis of CQDs, contemporarily preserving their homogeneity to bridge the gap between laboratory-scale synthesis and large-scale commercial production.

Considering the need to bridge the existing research gap and achieve highly scalable production of homogeneous and adjustable-shaped CQDs with exceptional quantum yield and selectivity, this study proposed a novel approach utilising the electrochemically-induced CO2 nanobubbles exfoliation method. This innovative method offers precise control over the size of the synthesised CQD by adjusting the electrolysis current and ensures scalability in production yield owing to the rapid rate of exfoliation facilitated by CO2 nanobubbles. This study also explored significant enhancements in the quantum yield of the exfoliated CQDs and their selectivity towards Fe3+ ions. Subsequently, these advancements were seamlessly integrated into a three-dimensional (3D)-printed portable detection system, capitalising on optical detection principles for real-time and on-site sensing of Fe3+ ions. The performance of the integrated system in terms of its sensitivity and reliability was evaluated, thereby establishing its efficacy in practical applications.

In short, this study encompasses a comprehensive approach covering characterisation, surface functionalisation, and morphological analysis to evaluate the efficacy of CO2 nanobubbles-assisted exfoliation for synthesising CQDs in an integrated portable detection system. The non-toxic nature of the CO2 nanobubbles-assisted exfoliation method opens up a novel avenue for synthesising advanced materials for sensing technologies and other responsive detection applications, thus contemporarily minimising negative environmental impacts.

HB-graded graphite rods (diameter of 2.0 mm) were purchased from RedCircle, while sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS, NaC12H25SO4), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), sulphuric acid (H2SO4), aluminium and kaolin were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Additionally, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (C10H16N2O8) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific, while 100 ppm standard stock solution of Fe3+, Cu2+, and Pb2+ was purchased from PerkinElmer. Besides, Chinese earthen clay was purchased from Keane Ceramics, while Grade 1 Whatman filter paper was purchased from Whatman. All chemicals and reagents utilised in the experiment were of analytical grade. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experimental procedure.

The nanoporous ceramic medium was prepared using a clay mixture composed of nanocellulose fibres as pore formers in a solid-state sintering process. Detailed information on the preparation of the nanoporous ceramic medium is provided in Supplementary Materials.

Approximately 10 g of SDS was dissolved in 150 mL of ultrapure water to form a standard electrolyte solution. A commercial pressurised CO2 tank purchased from a local aquarium store was then used to produce CO2 nanobubbles in the electrolyte via the prepared nanoporous ceramic medium at a rate of 1.5 L/min for 5 min. The presence of CO2 nanobubbles was examined via violet backscattering when the electrolyte was irradiated with an ultraviolet (UV) laser. Subsequently, two graphite rods were connected to a DC power supply and placed approximately 3 cm deep into the electrolyte. The experimental setup and the detection of CO2 nanobubbles are described further in Fig. S1 (Supplementary Materials).

The DC power supply was set at 0.3 A and 30 V for 40 min to allow the exfoliation of the graphite rods and synthesis of CQDs under room temperature conditions. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 10 min. Next, the supernatant solution was collected for dialysis using a 1000 Da dialysis tube (pre-activated using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and sodium bicarbonate) for 24 hrs. The fluorescence emission of the synthesised CQDs was analysed by estimating the image’s brightness after converting it into a grayscale bitmap image using the ImageJ digital image processing program. The fluorescence images were captured using a smartphone camera under an illumination wavelength of 405 nm without light exposure. The 1000 Da-dialysed solution was then freeze-dried to obtain solid CQDs. Detailed information on the characterisations and quantum yield calculation are presented in Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface functionalisation of the synthesised CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Around 15 mL of the 1000-Da dialysed CQDs solution was mixed with 20 μ L of ethylenediamine and 20 μ L of H2SO4. The reactants were transferred into a Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor and heated to 150 ℃ for 5 hrs. After the reactor was cooled in the fume hood for 30 min, the images of the resulting pale yellowish solution, which indicated the successfully implemented doping of the CQDs, were captured using a smartphone camera under an illumination wavelength of 405 nm without light exposure. The fluorescence emission analysis was performed by estimating the image brightness after converting the images into a grayscale bitmap image using the ImageJ software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heavy metals detection of doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quenching efficiency was determined by mixing the functionalised CQDs with synthetic heavy metal ions, namely, Fe3+, Cu2+, and Pb2+ at 100 ppm. The analysis was carried out by capturing the image of the mixture positioned in a cuvette using a smartphone camera under an illumination wavelength of 405 nm without light exposure. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission, F, or quenching efficiency, F/F
o , was analysed by estimating the image’s brightness after converting it into a grayscale bitmap image using the ImageJ software. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of a functionalised CQD solution without metal ions was used as the benchmark value, F
o . Subsequently, a correlation was established between the fluorescence quenching, F/F
o, and Fe3+ concentration from 10 to 100 ppm. Detailed information on the pH and temperature stability studies of doped CQDs are available in Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the 3D portable doped CQDs-based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The Computer-aided Design (CAD) program in SolidWorks software was employed to draw the proposed 3D portable smartphone-based heavy metal sensor model, which was composed of a mobile phone, cuvette, reflector (mirror), and UV light beam. The design was completed based on the appropriate measurements to construct the cavities to hold each component. The 3D design was saved in STL format and sent to a Cartesian 3D FDM printer (CR-10S), which was connected to Simplify3D as the slicing tool. Using polylactic acid (PLA) as the starting material, the fabrication was completed layer-by-layer according to the 3D design using the FDM method with a specified fabrication speed (60 mm/s), layer thickness (0.4 mm), nozzle temperature (210 ℃ ) and bed temperature (60 ℃ ). The dimension of the overall design was 25 cm × 10 cm × 15 cm.

Regarding the detection principle, approximately 2 mL of synthetic wastewater sample with a Fe3+ concentration from 1 to 10 ppm was placed into the cuvette containing 1 mL of doped CQDs. The sample was then illuminated using a 12 V laser beam at 300 nm. The fluorescence emitted by the analyte was reflected and directed by the mirror to the smartphone’s camera. The optical filter was specified with an optical density of OD 6, a bandwidth of 10 K, and wavelength detection at 540 nm (green). The captured fluorescence image was analysed with its fluorescence intensity, F, via RGB Colour Detector, an Android in-house colour recognition application, for Red-Green-Blue (RGB) analysis. The resulting F was validated by comparing it with the calibration results obtained using Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS). The fluorescence intensity of a CQD solution without Fe3+ ions was used as the benchmark value, F
o .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structural characterisations of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The HRTEM analysis showed that the prepared CQDs appeared as nearly spherical and well-dispersed morphology, as depicted in Fig. 1A. The repulsive interaction of the SDS anionic chains on top of the CQDs contributed to the dispersible colloidal properties that prevented them from agglomerating. Moreover, Fig. 1 B displays the structure of the CQDs, which possessed an amorphous biphasic carbon core with two discrete lattice fringes of 0.331 nm and 0.212 nm, which correspond to the carbon (002) and carbon (100) diffraction planes of the sp2 graphitic carbon [35,36]. This observation demonstrated that the CQDs should behave as a graphite material with a polycrystalline and multitudinous-dimensional structure. The result is also in line with the average size of CQDs from the collection of 100 CQD particles, which exhibits a diameter of roughly 2.24 ± 1.07 nm (Fig. 1C). Furthermore, the size of CQDs that mostly ranged 1–3 nm indicates the consistent energy that was supplied for carbonisation during electrolysis.

The surface functional groups, elemental composition, and thermophysical properties of the synthesised CQDs were analysed via Fourier-transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray Photoelectron (XPS) spectroscopy, and Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA), respectively, as shown in Supplementary Materials. The FTIR spectroscopy and XPS analysis revealed similar results, specifically the detection of polyenes of the CQDs’ structure, proving the successful nitrogen and sulphur doping process. Moreover, the TGA analysis recorded an endothermic pyrolysis decomposition trend, whereby the CQDs were relatively stable beyond 700 ℃ due to the presence of the remaining primitive core states of the graphite.

Conventional liquid-phase surfactant-based exfoliation is associated with certain advantages, such as the weakening of the attractive Van der Waals forces between the primitive graphene layers within the solvent, and they are ready to be deposited or integrated with other polymeric composites [37,38]. Nevertheless, this standard and simple method heavily relies on surfactants to grant remarkable dispersibility and colloidal stability of the two-dimensional (2D) graphene layers due to their low solubility in numerous surfactants, except for dimethylformamide (DMF) and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) [39,40]. Without a suitable surfactant selection, excessive heat and electricity will be required to initiate the exfoliation, which may possibly cause the graphite to disintegrate into unprecedented structural deformation. Therefore, external supporting forces, such as ultrasonication and electric field shearing, are a necessity. By incorporating CO2 nanobubbles, similar beneficial control over the shape and size of the exfoliated graphene layers can be achieved without depending on the surfactant. Besides, the CO2 nanobubbles can overcome the mass transfer limitations of the lateral orientation of graphene layers in graphite by forming dynamic cavities to vibrate and squeeze the graphitic templates.

Both the surfactant-based and CO2 nanobubble-assisted methods offer several pros and cons. For instance, the concentration of the surfactant in the surfactant-based method can be adjusted to produce CQDs with uniform size and structure. However, the quantum yield could be typically lower due to the capping micelles attributed to the strong and toxic surfactants used [41]. In terms of scalability, optimising the surfactant formulation will be challenging to ensure the reproducibility of the CQDs. In contrast, the CO2 nanobubble-assisted method can possibly control the size and morphology of the synthesised CQDs, simultaneously avoiding the use of strong cationic or anionic surfactants and assuring the dispersibility of exfoliated graphene layers to form CQDs. Intriguingly, the relatively small-sized reactor can be scaled up based on the modular concept to achieve the ideal production rate of CQDs for large-scale production.

The quantum yield of the synthesised CQDs was 26.17%, implying that electrolysis was a more facile and efficient method in disintegrating the graphite into graphene layers, allowing the carbonisation of graphene and formation of the CQDs. This claim was supported by a previous study, which synthesised CQDs with a quantum yield of 11.20% via the microwave-assisted method using SDS as the solvent [42]. Fig. 2A shows the ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectra of the CQDs produced at different currents, with an absorption peak of 296 nm, similar to that reported in past literature [42,43]. This peak could be ascribed to the n to π* transition of the CO bond. The increased absorption peaks associated with the CQDs produced from 0.1 A to 0.5 A might be due to the decrease in the size of the CQDs when prepared at a higher current value [45]. Smaller CQDs would cause light scattering, corroborating a feint of higher absorbance value. However, the reduced size was insufficient to trigger a significant hop in the energy level, which could register different wavelength absorption. Meanwhile, the narrowing appearance of the absorption peaks could be ascribed to the altered properties of the CQDs into a more homogenised shape and structure under the current effect. In brief, the irregular edges and peripherals of the CQDs were disintegrated due to the higher current applied during the process.

Compared to Fig. 2B, the excitation peak at 210 nm reflects the π to π* electronic transition of the CC bond, as reported in previous studies [19,46–49]. Besides, the emission peak wavelength of the CQDs at 472 nm displayed a significant Stoke shift of 262 nm, which enabled a better recognition and detection of the target emission wavelength from the noise (background signal). Typically, the fluorescence origin of the CQDs was elucidated based on the varying surface defects, where the surface functional groups formed non-perfect sp2 or sp3 domains that act as surface energy traps [46,49]. It was also possible for the CQDs to undergo bandgap transition of conjugated π-domains. As such, the sp3 matrix could encapsulate the π-domains isolated from the sp2 islands to serve as the energy trap [6]. However, the CQDs synthesised in this study demonstrated a wavelength-excitation-independent phenomenon, which hypothesises the fluorescence origin as a result of the invalid surface defects. The fluorescence origin of the CQDs synthesised through this electrochemical oxidation method was most likely derived from the quantum confinement theory, in which the π to π* electronic transition of the carbon skeleton acquired from graphite (the primitive material) was dominant in this fluorescence phenomenon [43]. A similar hypothesis of the fluorescence origin could be found in other works [43,50]. Additionally, the similar absorption peak trend developed by the CQDs at varying currents could be used to support the above statement, as they all exhibited identical absorption peaks ascribed to their consistent particle size and functional groups adhered to the surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective sensing of Fe3+ ions by doped CQDs and its application in real-time and on-site sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
The prevalence of Fe3+, Cu2+, and Pb2+ represent the broad spectrum of metal ion concentrations naturally found in Borneo river water. This study applied the selectivity test of CQDs in both synthetic and environmental wastewater samples [51,52]. Upon adding 100 ppm of Fe3+ ions, the F/F
o value was significantly lower than other metal ions (Cu2+ and Pb2+), indicating that the prepared doped CQDs had a higher affinity (selectivity) to bind with the Fe3+ ions ( Fig. 3A). This finding corresponds with those found in past literature findings, in which the chelating process incurred between the Fe3+ ions and the surface functionalities, such as carbonyl, carboxyl, and amino groups [19,48,53]. The oxygen atom in these groups might donate an electron pair to fill in the vacancies on the Fe3+ orbital. Fig. 3B displays another observation from the UV-Vis spectra of different doped CQDs/metal ion complexes. Accordingly, the absorption peak of the doped CQDs/Fe3+ was more significant than the other metal ion complexes. Hence, this observation could be used to comprehend the affinity of the doped CQDs towards the Fe3+ ions under the same added concentration.

Consequently, the intricate and complex doped CQDs/Fe3+ would result in a non-radiative structure, forming the basis for measuring the amount of metal ions, similar to those claimed in previous studies [54,55]. Moreover, the inner filter effect [49] could be induced between the Fe3+ ions and doped CQDs, where the fluorescence wavelength of the doped CQDs overlaps with the absorption wavelength of the Fe3+ ions [8,56]. The Fe3+ ions would then absorb the energy emitted by the exciton of the doped CQDs, causing no fluorescence emission when the exciton returns to its ground state [49]. In other words, a higher concentration of metal ions added to the doped CQDs results in a lower F/F
o value, indicating a better quenching efficiency (Fig. 3C). The affinity and selectivity of the doped CQDs to be quenched by the Fe3+ could be proven using the same plot by examining the quenching quantity of the doped CQDs when added with 100 ppm of Fe3+ ions. Based on the results, approximately 80% of the fluorescence emission was quenched. The linear response of the calibration curve in the range of 0–20 ppm also suggested that the Fe3+ ions efficiently quenched the doped CQDs.

Fig. 3D shows the photo-stability of the doped CQDs in five pH levels (3–11). When the doped CQDs reacted with the different Fe3+ concentrations, no significant change in the fluorescence emission trend was observed, which was in accordance with previous literature. Although the pH shifted from a strong acidic to a strong basic condition, the blue-shifting or red-shifting of the doped CQDs’ fluorescence emission recorded no obvious observation [43]. The robust stability of the doped CQDs under different pH conditions might be due to the inertness of the graphite that served as the primitive material during the CQD synthesis [57]. In line with the literature, the higher affinity of the doped CQDs that react with the Fe3+ ions via electron transfer could resist the typical effect of different pH levels that would generally affect the fluorescence emission as a result of the protonation or deprotonation effect on the binding structure of the doped CQDs [44,50,58]. In addition, the π to π* and n to π* electronic transitions could be depleted through the utilisation of the H+ and OH- ions in acidic or basic environments to support the fluorescence origin claim. As such, the π to π* and n to π* electronic transitions of the surface functional groups, such as CC and CO, were not responsible for the fluorescence emission of the doped CQDs [59].

Furthermore, Fig. 3E depicts that the doped CQDs were relatively stable under different temperatures. However, the fluorescence intensity possessed by the doped CQDs showed a slight difference at various temperature conditions. It was observed that the quenching efficiency increased with increasing temperature. Previous studies proved that this trend could give insight into the quenching mechanism of the doped CQDs and Fe3+ ions in a dynamic mode, known explicitly as collision quenching [56,60,61]. The proposed quenching mechanism states that the radiative recombination of the doped CQDs and a specific metal ion occurs via collisions in an excited mode (illumination) based on the photoinduced electron transfer. A higher temperature would possibly provide more kinetic energy for the collision between the doped CQDs and Fe3+ ions, indicating a more intense molecular activity. Thus, the sensing performance was unaffected by the varying pH levels owing to the homogeneity of the doped CQDs in terms of size and structural morphology. In addition, the temperature stability test revealed the stability of the doped CQDs/Fe3+ to perform dynamic fluorescence quenching under various temperatures. Future works should consider demonstrating the stability tests under different conditions, such as humidity, and adding any competing heavy metal ions or any competing organic molecules in the sample matrix for bioimaging or biosensing.

In this present work, the efficacy of the Fe3+ ion detection based on fluorescence quenching was attributed to the presence of strong hydrophilic and active functionalities, such as -OH and -COO, in the doped CQDs. The strong affinity between these organic functionalities induced the chelation and aggregation of the doped CQDs with the Fe3+ ions [28,29,62]. The unique coordination interaction between the aforementioned functional groups, especially -OH and Fe3+ ions, could trigger the precipitation of insoluble residues, leading to the structural and morphological transition of the doped CQDs. This reaction is associated with a mechanism similar to the qualitative and quantitative detection of Fe3+ ions in wastewater. Nitrogen and sulphur doping also contributed to the interactions with the Fe3+ ions due to their rich electron density. In terms of fluorescence detection, the light irradiation caused the photoexcited electrons to collide and travel from the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) to the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO), resulting in the fluorescence emission once the electrons returned to their initial states.

However, when Fe3+ ions were introduced, the aggregated doped CQDs/Fe3+ complex blocked the photoexcited electrons from travelling back to their ground state. Instead, they formed the complexation with the vacant molecular orbitals of the Fe3+ ions, such as the 3d orbital. For instance, the presence of five half-filled orbitals in the 3d orbital of Fe3+ ions suggests their strong tendency and ability to uphold and capture excited electrons from their excited state [63–65]. In comparison, Cu2+ and Pb2+ ions possess electron configurations of [Ar]3d9 and [Xe]6s24f145d10, respectively. The former ion only had one vacancy in the 3d orbital, while the latter ion only had two vacancies in the 6p orbital. Furthermore, the electrostatic force of attraction from the nuclear shell of the atom might be too weak to form bond interactions at these orbitals, reducing the tendency to be selectively bonded with the doped CQDs, as examined with the selectivity tests above. Consequently, the photoinduced charge transfer was restrained, and quenching occurred. Therefore, the doped CQDs possess an affinity to bind with the metal ions in the following sequence, i.e., Fe3+, Cu2+, and Pb2+.

In short, the interaction of the synthesised doped CQDs with other heavy metal ions will possibly rely on the electron acceptance capability of the grafted surface functional groups on the doped CQDs, as well as the charge of the metal ions that determines their electron configurations at the oxidation states, resulting in their tendency to accept electrons. These combining factors will form the basis of the affinity of the CQDs to undergo chelation and coordination interactions with the particular metal ions. However, it should be noted that the formation of ligands from the metal ions and doped CQDs will possess repulsive force as it agglomerates, therefore also affecting the selectivity. The net force obtained from both the electron transfer and repulsive force between the doped CQDs and metal ions will ultimately determine the selectivity of the particular metal ions to be bonded. Apart from that, the SO bonds in the CQDs could probably enhance the complexation formation with the metal ions due to the presence of free electron pairs. The functionalities that were rich in electrons would serve as electron donors to facilitate the formation of coordination complexes by transferring the lone pairs of electrons to the metal ions. Therefore, the presence of SO would likely increase the probability and stability of the metal ion complexation.

In addressing the objective of real-time and on-site sensing of heavy metal ions, a single 3D miniaturised sensor system was embedded with an RGB analysis application, namely, RGB Colour Detector, to analyse the intensity of the green luminescence emitted by the samples ( Fig. 4A). As presented in Fig. 4B, the normalised fluorescence intensity exhibited excellent linearity (R2) of 0.9932 in the studied range of Fe3+ concentration. Meanwhile, the miniaturised system’s limit of detection (LOD) was 1.89 ppm (multiplied by 3.3 with the standard deviation of the y-intercept and divided by the calibration plot’s slope) [14,48,49]. The LOD was comparable with the results in previous studies that quantified Fe3+ ions using a CQDs-based sensor [24–26,66,67]. The smallest detectable change in Fe3+ concentration sensitivity was approximately 0.1 ppm.

The plot in Fig. 4C shows that the quenching efficiency of a quencher should fit the Stern-Volmer equation, as expressed in Eq. (1):
(1)

sv denotes the Stern-Volmer constant (ppm−1), and Q represents the quencher concentration [68]. The 3D miniaturised sensor system exhibited the Stern-Volmer plot acquired linearity (R2) of 0.9525 over the Fe3+ concentration range of 1–10 ppm. Besides, K
sv is referred to as the slope of the plot, which equals 0.1782, indicating the product of the quenching rate constant of the fluorescence lifetime of the doped CQDs. The Fe3+ concentration below 1 ppm could not be quantified based on the fluorescence response since the linearity between 0 and 1 ppm was low (0.9525). This finding corresponds to the LOD value, which was higher than 1 ppm. However, the result was relatively higher than those reported in several studies [35,69]. The probable reason for such a result might be due to the dust particles in the sample analyte that disrupt the magnitude of the emitted fluorescence signal.

Lastly, the performance of the 3D miniaturised sensor system to quantify Fe3+ concentration in actual samples was assessed using a raw wastewater sample that was collected from a discharge drain at Curtin University Malaysia. The concentration of Fe3+ ions in the wastewater sample was validated using the AAS technique, which recorded an error of approximately 4.20% and was comparable with the previous studies that fabricated CQDs-based sensors for Fe3+ ion determination in different water sources [26,65,66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of graphite’s exfoliation with CO2 nanobubbles
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of CO2 nanobubbles on the synthesis of CQDs through electrolysis was validated by measuring the CQD yield (Fig. S4A in Supplementary Materials). Based on the results, the yield of CQDs using the nanoporous ceramic medium with CO2 nanobubbles was approximately 324%. In comparison, the number of visible black colour dots (graphene) observed in the beaker without CO2 nanobubbles was relatively low after 20 min of electrolysis (Fig. S4B in Supplementary Materials). Meanwhile, the improved exfoliation rate of graphite due to the CO2 nanobubbles could be observed during the electrochemical synthesis, as many black-coloured dots (graphene) were dispersed in the beaker (Fig. S4C in Supplementary Materials). In line with previous literature, it is reasonable to suggest that the presence of CO2 nanobubbles could enhance the interfacial forces and their diffusion probability between the gas-liquid interface to develop a pseudo-template between the spaces around the graphite rods for exfoliating the graphene layers [20,70]. For instance, the high stability of the CO2 nanobubbles would function as a “wedge” to squeeze into the spatial interface of the layered graphene [23]. Thus, a greater disintegration of graphite would induce the yield of CQDs more significantly.

Previous studies have also shown that poor surface tension of the liquid-liquid interface was undesirable for graphene exfoliation [20,21]. However, the use of anionic SDS surfactant in this study had a negligible effect on the exfoliation of graphite rods into the graphene layers. The reduced surface tension due to the use of surfactant might cause the SDS micelles to be primarily isolated between the liquid-liquid interface than in the spatial region between the graphene layers of the graphite [71], thus causing them to squeeze out from the graphene layers. Nevertheless, the presence of the CO2 nanobubbles would still induce a driving force, known as the steric hindrance effect, in which the repulsive interaction between the CO2 nanobubbles accompanied by the enhanced electrostatic stabilisation due to the anionic property of SDS will support the exfoliation of the graphene layers, which agrees with past simulation studies [22,72]. In this regard, the utilised CO2 nanobubbles in this study had overcome the Van der Waals interaction that holds the graphene layers by sustaining a considerable repulsive force. However, the pre-requisite for such exfoliation to occur must be accompanied by the inherent repulsive interaction of the anionic property of SDS in order to expand the spatial region between the graphene layers, which was inconceivably small (around 6–10 Å) [21,23,73].

Hypothetically, the rough surface of the graphite indicates the protuberances as a result of the uneven layering of graphene layers ( Fig. 5A), which were selectively electrolysed. The progressively smoothened graphite surface after the reaction was evidenced by the elongation and flattening of the uneven surfaces, as depicted in Fig. 5B. These features imply that the microstructures could become less porous, and the remaining fractals were approximately spherical. The increased sphericity demonstrates the formation of rounder microstructures due to the more uniform and homogenous exfoliation in the presence of CO2 nanobubbles. In fact, the nanobubbles’ ability to uphold their spherical shape to preserve the optimum energy absorption structure would allow the graphene to be exfoliated in a less angular manner [70,74]. Meanwhile, the extension of the grain area indicates that the exfoliation of the graphene layers from the graphite rods was attributed to the synergistic anionic repulsion of the SDS molecules and the intercalation of CO2 nanobubbles via the formation of pseudo-templates instead of reacting with the graphite rods merely by current driving force to remove the graphene in tiny and irregular particles.

To further support the above claims regarding the surface morphology of the exfoliated graphite, the morphological images were converted into 8-bit grayscale images using ImageJ and subjected to grain boundary analysis using MATLAB PolyLX toolbox. This technique involves an object-oriented standard image analysis protocol that processes and visualises the microstructural images to return measurable particle shape properties [75]. The image background was suppressed by applying a grayscale threshold, resulting in the binarisation of images via the adjustment of the area of interest. The removal of the image background in pixel values was also to ensure the reproducibility of particle images. Subsequently, the generated particle images were analysed via the MATLAB PolyLX toolbox to obtain specific parameters, such as the average length of the longest axis (length), the average length of the shortest axis (width), average area, and average parameter, as tabulated in Table S1 (Supplementary Materials). The MATLAB PolyLX toolbox also determined the average Grain Shape Factor (GSF), average Grain Shape Index (GSI), and average roundness (R) according to the mathematical formulations defined in Eq. (2), Eq. (3), and Eq. (4), as follows:
(2)

where L indicates the average length (Pixel), W represents the average width (Pixel), P refers to the average perimeter (Pixel), and A denotes the average area (Pixel).

The GSF value could be comprehended as the ellipticity or fractal dimension of the particles, which indicates any deviation from the circular shape of the particles. Meanwhile, the GSI value describes the minimum value approximate to the π value for a circular proximate particle [76]. Table S1 shows that the average GSF decreased after exfoliation, indicating the transition of the protuberances into elongated and flattened surfaces. Conversely, the average GSI increased and approximated the π value, which corresponds to the increment of the average R-value. Both trends supported the claim whereby the CO2 nanobubbles played a significant role in producing the graphene layers with less angularity due to their inherent spherical shapes, which served as the intercalation medium of the graphene layers.

Given the successful synthesis of CQDs with excellent selectivity on heavy metal ions, the next challenge is to scale up and implement the synthesis process for full commercialisation. Although the application of CO2 nanobubbles enhances the gas and mass transfer due to their superior solubility and stability in the solvent, the production rate of CO2 nanobubbles via the same propulsion energy in scale-up systems might be insufficient to achieve the same mass transfer effect in the solvent for exfoliating the graphite. Additionally, the electrolyte composition that could sustain the stable surface tension for the nanobubble accommodation might be different. Besides, the use of a larger reactor size requires larger carbon electrodes to produce the desired CQDs. Consequently, the contact surface area of the electrodes with the nanobubbles may be reduced due to the decrement of the overall surface area-to-volume ratio. Extra caution should also be given to provide additional control and automation over such large reactor volumes, which consume a significant amount of electricity and more heat generation during the reaction, leading to further concerns over the energy efficiency, safety, and cost of the production system. There is also a need to consider a precise strategy to purify the resulting bulk CQDs.

Instead of scaling up the reactor size, the scalability of the novel synthesis method is now focused on building multiple electrolysis reactors. In view of the growing demand to accomplish a higher production rate of CQDs, the development of a modular system will be necessary to offer flexibility and ease of maintenance when any repairment activities are needed, allowing the particular faulty module or reactor to be isolated from the overall system, thus minimising the downtime period. A similar concept can be applied to the defective module, as it can be independently deployed for subsequent operation. Since research is still ongoing, process optimisation and technology innovation can be initiated and implemented in individual modules to allow a seamless integration into the overall reaction.

In general, CO2 is not inherently toxic to living things except beyond its permissible threshold limit. To date, there have been myriad studies that showed interest in utilising CO2 as a catalyst to achieve practical environmental remediation or climate change mitigation. The adoption of smaller CO2 nanobubbles indicates better resource efficiency for the production of valuable materials. In this study, the CO2 nanobubbles were generated through the nanoporous ceramic medium in the electrolyte to maintain their colloidal stability. The well-ventilated area proximate to this synthesis reaction would minimise the effect of accumulation and replacement of oxygen by CO2, which could lead to an oxygen-deficient atmosphere. Over time, the nanobubbles tend to coalesce and agglomerate to form bigger bubbles, restraining their presence in electrolytes. Other possible factors that could reduce the stability of nanobubbles include surface tension, temperature, and pressure of the dispersion medium, which is worth further exploration.

The present work successfully synthesised CQDs from graphite rods via an integrated CO2 nanobubble-enhanced electrolysis method. Under detailed optical and structural characterisation, the synthesised CQDs possessed a desirable Stoke shift of 262 nm, indicating their promising role in preserving a significant detectable signal in sensing applications. It was intriguing to observe that the CQDs were excitation-wavelength independent. Therefore, their fluorescence origin was ascribed to the π to π* transition (quantum confinement effect) inherited from the graphite rods, which served as the raw material in this synthesis process. The aforementioned fluorescence origin claim was further proven through the stable fluorescence of the doped CQDs under varying pH levels. Moreover, the temperature-dependent fluorescence detection of Fe3+ was ascribed to the dynamic quenching between the Fe3+ ions and doped CQDs. The quenching efficiency was improved when more kinetic energy was supplied to the detection environment. In terms of the practicality of the proposed system for real-time and on-site Fe3+ ion detection, the 3D-printed miniaturised system composed of doped CQDs and a portable phone camera recorded a linearity (R2) of 0.9525 in the Stern-Volmer plot and a sensitivity of 0.1 ppm. The results represent an approximate confidence level of 95%, in which the Fe3+ concentration level could explain the fluorescence quenching. Lastly, it was found that the presence of CO2 nanobubbles greatly improved the exfoliation rate, resulting in the exfoliated graphite becoming more elongated and flattened. The findings indicate that the inherent spherical shape of the nanobubbles successfully intercalated the graphene layers in a more homogenised manner compared to the formation of irregular graphene when employing low current-induced electrolysis.

In summary, this study introduced a scalable synthesis method that offers a significantly improved rate and production yield compared to conventional surfactant-based exfoliation. In addition, this approach provides better control over the size and shape of the synthesised CQDs. The use of non-toxic CO2 nanobubbles during the CQD synthesis also suggests broader applications of this method, such as developing sensors to detect heavy metal ions in water samples. Nevertheless, the present work only focused on heavy metal ion detection as one of the potential environmental applications of the CQDs. Other practical applications of the synthesised CQDs include fluorescence multimodal sensing platforms with different sensing mechanisms, as well as biocompatible imaging and detection of organic pollutants or molecules. With the enhanced modifications of the CQDs, they can potentially be integrated into responsive sensors for gas detection and energy harvesting through photocatalytic reactions. Ultimately, further exploration of the potential utilisation of the CQDs in environmental and healthcare sectors, such as wearable devices or photosensitive materials, could address the current challenges in sensing technology.

Pau Loke Show: Validation, Investigation. Man Kee Lam: Validation, Investigation. Bryant Wei Liang Ng: Investigation. Yu Dong Yat: Writing – original draft, Project administration. Inn Shi Tan: Writing – review & editing. Henry Chee Yew Foo: Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.
